title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 05

questions.prompt: Originalized court chronicle, Delhi Sultanate, c. 1325: “The sultan ordered the revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators. Persian scribes prepared the registers, while judges heard disputes in the market quarter. Commanders were granted assignments so that cavalry would be ready when summoned.” What was the most likely purpose of the chronicle?

choices.A: To argue that the sultanate was governed by independent merchant guilds
choices.B: To show that South Asia had no bureaucratic traditions after 1200
choices.C: To present the sultan as a ruler who maintained taxation, justice, and military order
choices.D: To promote the replacement of agrarian taxation with factory wages

questions.correctAnswerText: To present the sultan as a ruler who maintained taxation, justice, and military order
questions.explanation: The chronicle emphasizes revenue officers, judges, registers, commanders, and cavalry service under the sultan’s authority.

questions.prompt: Originalized court chronicle, Delhi Sultanate, c. 1325: “The sultan ordered the revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators. Persian scribes prepared the registers, while judges heard disputes in the market quarter. Commanders were granted assignments so that cavalry would be ready when summoned.” Which broader context best explains the presence of Persian scribes in the court?

choices.A: Persianate administrative and literary culture influenced many Islamic states in South Asia.
choices.B: Persian became the language of industrial factory management in South Asia.
choices.C: South Asian rulers rejected all written administrative traditions.
choices.D: Persian scribes were used only in European maritime empires.

questions.correctAnswerText: Persianate administrative and literary culture influenced many Islamic states in South Asia.
questions.explanation: Persian scribes reflect the spread of Persianate administrative culture within Islamic-ruled South Asian states.

questions.prompt: Originalized court chronicle, Delhi Sultanate, c. 1325: “The sultan ordered the revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators. Persian scribes prepared the registers, while judges heard disputes in the market quarter. Commanders were granted assignments so that cavalry would be ready when summoned.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the state depended on agrarian revenue?

choices.A: “Judges heard disputes”
choices.B: “Persian scribes”
choices.C: “Cavalry would be ready”
choices.D: “Revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators”
questions.explanation: Measuring fields and recording cultivators directly indicates agrarian taxation.

questions.prompt: Originalized court chronicle, Delhi Sultanate, c. 1325: “The sultan ordered the revenue officers to measure the fields and record the cultivators. Persian scribes prepared the registers, while judges heard disputes in the market quarter. Commanders were granted assignments so that cavalry would be ready when summoned.” The passage most directly suggests which feature of the Delhi Sultanate?

choices.A: The disappearance of rural cultivators from political life
choices.B: The interaction of military elites, administrators, judges, and agricultural producers
choices.C: The replacement of Islamic law by elected village councils
choices.D: The isolation of South Asia from wider Islamic cultural traditions

questions.correctAnswerText: The interaction of military elites, administrators, judges, and agricultural producers
questions.explanation: The passage shows commanders, scribes, judges, revenue officers, and cultivators interacting within the sultanate’s political order.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Mayapan in the Yucatán, c. 1250–1450: “A walled city contained temples, elite residences, markets, and neighborhoods linked to nearby maize fields. Archaeologists have found obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels.” Which interpretation is best supported by the description?

choices.A: Mayapan was a purely nomadic settlement without agriculture.
choices.B: Mayapan depended entirely on European trade before 1450.
choices.C: Mayapan combined urban political organization, agriculture, and regional exchange.
choices.D: Mayapan lacked social hierarchy and ceremonial architecture.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mayapan combined urban political organization, agriculture, and regional exchange.
questions.explanation: Walls, temples, elite residences, maize fields, markets, obsidian, and cacao show political organization, farming, and exchange.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Mayapan in the Yucatán, c. 1250–1450: “A walled city contained temples, elite residences, markets, and neighborhoods linked to nearby maize fields. Archaeologists have found obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels.” Which evidence best supports the claim that Mayapan participated in regional trade?

choices.A: “Obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels”
choices.B: “Nearby maize fields”
choices.C: “A walled city”
choices.D: “Elite residences”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels”
questions.explanation: Obsidian from highland sources and cacao residues indicate goods moving through regional exchange networks.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Mayapan in the Yucatán, c. 1250–1450: “A walled city contained temples, elite residences, markets, and neighborhoods linked to nearby maize fields. Archaeologists have found obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels.” The presence of temples and markets most directly reflects which broader pattern in the Americas before 1450?

choices.A: The replacement of urban life by pastoral nomadism
choices.B: The disappearance of ritual centers after 1200
choices.C: The absence of interregional exchange in Mesoamerica
choices.D: The development of cities where religious, political, and commercial functions overlapped

questions.correctAnswerText: The development of cities where religious, political, and commercial functions overlapped
questions.explanation: Temples, elite residences, and markets show overlapping religious, political, and economic functions.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Mayapan in the Yucatán, c. 1250–1450: “A walled city contained temples, elite residences, markets, and neighborhoods linked to nearby maize fields. Archaeologists have found obsidian blades from highland sources and cacao residues in vessels.” Obsidian blades most directly illustrate which relationship between technology and exchange?

choices.A: Metallurgy ended the use of stone tools in all regions before 1200.
choices.B: Specialized tools and valued materials could circulate through regional exchange networks.
choices.C: The Americas imported all tools from Afro-Eurasia before 1450.
choices.D: Obsidian was used only in oceanic navigation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Specialized tools and valued materials could circulate through regional exchange networks.
questions.explanation: Obsidian blades show that valued materials and specialized tools moved through exchange systems.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the central Sahara c. 1300 marks caravan routes linking Sijilmasa, Taghaza, Takedda, and Hausa towns. Symbols show salt blocks moving south, copper and textiles moving across oasis towns, and camel caravans stopping near wells.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Desert environments shaped trade through oases, wells, and camel transport.
choices.B: Saharan trade depended on steam-powered railroads.
choices.C: Hausa towns were isolated from transregional exchange.
choices.D: Salt moved mainly from forest regions to Mediterranean ports.

questions.correctAnswerText: Desert environments shaped trade through oases, wells, and camel transport.
questions.explanation: The map shows wells, oasis towns, and camel caravans structuring trade across the Sahara.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the central Sahara c. 1300 marks caravan routes linking Sijilmasa, Taghaza, Takedda, and Hausa towns. Symbols show salt blocks moving south, copper and textiles moving across oasis towns, and camel caravans stopping near wells.” Which broader context best explains the pattern shown?

choices.A: The end of all commerce between North Africa and the Sahel
choices.B: The expansion of Atlantic plantation economies into the Sahara
choices.C: The importance of trans-Saharan routes in linking North African, Saharan, and West African economies
choices.D: The decline of camel transport after the spread of container shipping

questions.correctAnswerText: The importance of trans-Saharan routes in linking North African, Saharan, and West African economies
questions.explanation: The routes connect North African, Saharan, and Hausa commercial centers.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the central Sahara c. 1300 marks caravan routes linking Sijilmasa, Taghaza, Takedda, and Hausa towns. Symbols show salt blocks moving south, copper and textiles moving across oasis towns, and camel caravans stopping near wells.” A later state that sought to tax or control similar Sahelian routes would most likely have been interested in which goal?

choices.A: Eliminating Islam from all West African cities
choices.B: Increasing revenue from caravan commerce and regional markets
choices.C: Replacing caravan trade with Atlantic steamships before 1450
choices.D: Ending all contact between pastoral and urban communities

questions.correctAnswerText: Increasing revenue from caravan commerce and regional markets
questions.explanation: States could tax and control trade routes, oasis towns, and market connections.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the central Sahara c. 1300 marks caravan routes linking Sijilmasa, Taghaza, Takedda, and Hausa towns. Symbols show salt blocks moving south, copper and textiles moving across oasis towns, and camel caravans stopping near wells.” Which feature most directly supports the claim that states could profit from geographic chokepoints?

choices.A: The presence of Hausa towns
choices.B: The movement of textiles
choices.C: The use of camels
choices.D: The caravan stops near wells and oasis towns

questions.correctAnswerText: The caravan stops near wells and oasis towns
questions.explanation: Wells and oases were strategic points where states or local authorities could tax or regulate caravans.

questions.prompt: Originalized administrative manual, Khanate of Bukhara, c. 1600: “The amir’s officials shall inspect canal repairs before harvest. The qadi shall judge disputes in the bazaar, and the tribal commanders shall provide horsemen when the frontier summons arrive. Taxes on cotton and caravan goods are to be entered in the ledger.” What was the most likely purpose of the manual?

choices.A: To coordinate irrigation, justice, military service, and taxation under the ruler’s authority
choices.B: To abolish Islamic courts in Central Asian cities
choices.C: To replace all cavalry forces with European infantry
choices.D: To end commerce in caravan goods

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate irrigation, justice, military service, and taxation under the ruler’s authority
questions.explanation: The manual covers canals, qadi courts, tribal cavalry, taxes, and ledgers.

questions.prompt: Originalized administrative manual, Khanate of Bukhara, c. 1600: “The amir’s officials shall inspect canal repairs before harvest. The qadi shall judge disputes in the bazaar, and the tribal commanders shall provide horsemen when the frontier summons arrive. Taxes on cotton and caravan goods are to be entered in the ledger.” Which phrase most directly shows the state’s concern with managing the environment for agriculture?

choices.A: “The qadi shall judge disputes”
choices.B: “Tribal commanders”
choices.C: “Inspect canal repairs before harvest”
choices.D: “Caravan goods”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Inspect canal repairs before harvest”
questions.explanation: Canal maintenance reflects state concern with water management and agriculture.

questions.prompt: Originalized administrative manual, Khanate of Bukhara, c. 1600: “The amir’s officials shall inspect canal repairs before harvest. The qadi shall judge disputes in the bazaar, and the tribal commanders shall provide horsemen when the frontier summons arrive. Taxes on cotton and caravan goods are to be entered in the ledger.” Which broader context best explains the role of the qadi in the manual?

choices.A: Buddhist monastic courts governed all Central Asian cities after 1500.
choices.B: Islamic legal institutions remained important in many Central Asian states.
choices.C: European merchants replaced local judges throughout Central Asia.
choices.D: Secular democratic courts spread across the region before 1450.

questions.correctAnswerText: Islamic legal institutions remained important in many Central Asian states.
questions.explanation: A qadi was an Islamic judge, showing the role of Islamic law in Bukhara’s governance.

questions.prompt: Originalized administrative manual, Khanate of Bukhara, c. 1600: “The amir’s officials shall inspect canal repairs before harvest. The qadi shall judge disputes in the bazaar, and the tribal commanders shall provide horsemen when the frontier summons arrive. Taxes on cotton and caravan goods are to be entered in the ledger.” Which statement best evaluates the political system described?

choices.A: It depended only on elected urban assemblies.
choices.B: It combined sedentary administration, Islamic legal practice, and tribal military service.
choices.C: It rejected agriculture and commerce as sources of state power.
choices.D: It avoided written records in taxation.

questions.correctAnswerText: It combined sedentary administration, Islamic legal practice, and tribal military service.
questions.explanation: The manual links irrigation and tax records, qadi courts, and tribal cavalry service.

questions.prompt: Described royal object, Kingdom of Kongo, sixteenth century: “A carved cross combines Christian imagery with local symbols of kingship. A nearby inscription names a Kongo ruler using a Christian baptismal name while also listing his authority over provincial chiefs.” Which interpretation is best supported by the object?

choices.A: Kongo rulers adapted Christianity to local political traditions.
choices.B: Christianity eliminated all African forms of political authority.
choices.C: Kongo had no contact with Atlantic religious exchange.
choices.D: Portuguese missionaries governed Kongo without local rulers.

questions.correctAnswerText: Kongo rulers adapted Christianity to local political traditions.
questions.explanation: The cross combines Christian imagery with local kingship symbols and provincial authority.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch East India Company spice regulations in the Banda Islands, simplified pattern
Policy | Intended effect
Nutmeg trees counted by company agents | Monitor production
Local rulers required to sell through company contracts | Limit competition
Fortified posts placed near harbors | Enforce monopoly trade
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: The company encouraged unrestricted competition among all Asian merchants.
choices.B: Spice production was unrelated to European maritime expansion.
choices.C: The company ended its involvement in political control after 1600.
choices.D: Chartered companies used contracts, surveillance, and force to control commodity production.

questions.correctAnswerText: Chartered companies used contracts, surveillance, and force to control commodity production.
questions.explanation: Counting trees, company contracts, and fortified posts show surveillance, monopoly contracts, and coercive power.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch East India Company spice regulations in the Banda Islands, simplified pattern
Policy | Intended effect
Nutmeg trees counted by company agents | Monitor production
Local rulers required to sell through company contracts | Limit competition
Fortified posts placed near harbors | Enforce monopoly trade
Which evidence best supports the claim that the company exercised political power, not only commercial activity?

choices.A: “Nutmeg trees”
choices.B: “Fortified posts placed near harbors”
choices.C: “Monitor production”
choices.D: “Company contracts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Fortified posts placed near harbors”
questions.explanation: Fortified posts show military-political power used to enforce commercial goals.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch East India Company spice regulations in the Banda Islands, simplified pattern
Policy | Intended effect
Nutmeg trees counted by company agents | Monitor production
Local rulers required to sell through company contracts | Limit competition
Fortified posts placed near harbors | Enforce monopoly trade
Which broader environmental and economic context best explains the company’s interest in the Banda Islands?

choices.A: The Banda Islands were major sources of valuable spices that grew in limited ecological zones.
choices.B: The islands produced coal for European steam engines.
choices.C: The islands were the center of Atlantic sugar plantation production.
choices.D: Nutmeg cultivation required Arctic conditions.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Banda Islands were major sources of valuable spices that grew in limited ecological zones.
questions.explanation: Nutmeg’s limited growing zones made the islands highly valuable to spice traders.

questions.prompt:
Table: Dutch East India Company spice regulations in the Banda Islands, simplified pattern
Policy | Intended effect
Nutmeg trees counted by company agents | Monitor production
Local rulers required to sell through company contracts | Limit competition
Fortified posts placed near harbors | Enforce monopoly trade
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: European maritime commerce could transform local political relationships in Asian island societies.
choices.B: Asian rulers always prevented European companies from gaining influence.
choices.C: Spice monopolies were maintained only through voluntary consumer demand.
choices.D: European companies avoided all military infrastructure in Asia.

questions.correctAnswerText: European maritime commerce could transform local political relationships in Asian island societies.
questions.explanation: Company contracts, production monitoring, and fortifications altered local authority and trade.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony by a maroon leader in Jamaica, c. 1735: “We took to the mountains because the cane fields broke our bodies. The governor now offers paper promises, but our people know the paths and will not return to the whip. We ask land, freedom, and peace.” What was the most likely purpose of the testimony?

choices.A: To defend the plantation system as a source of liberty
choices.B: To encourage enslaved people to return voluntarily to sugar estates
choices.C: To explain resistance to slavery and negotiate recognition of maroon autonomy
choices.D: To request European settlement in the Jamaican highlands

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain resistance to slavery and negotiate recognition of maroon autonomy
questions.explanation: The testimony explains flight, resistance, mountain refuge, and demands for land, freedom, and peace.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony by a maroon leader in Jamaica, c. 1735: “We took to the mountains because the cane fields broke our bodies. The governor now offers paper promises, but our people know the paths and will not return to the whip. We ask land, freedom, and peace.” Which phrase best supports the claim that geography aided resistance?

choices.A: “Our people know the paths”
choices.B: “Paper promises”
choices.C: “The cane fields”
choices.D: “The governor”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Our people know the paths”
questions.explanation: Knowledge of mountain paths indicates how geography aided maroon resistance.

questions.prompt: Originalized testimony by a maroon leader in Jamaica, c. 1735: “We took to the mountains because the cane fields broke our bodies. The governor now offers paper promises, but our people know the paths and will not return to the whip. We ask land, freedom, and peace.” The testimony most directly illustrates which broader feature of Atlantic slavery?

choices.A: Plantation systems prevented all forms of resistance.
choices.B: Colonial governors always abolished slavery when challenged.
choices.C: Maroon communities disappeared before the eighteenth century.
choices.D: Enslaved and formerly enslaved people created forms of resistance and negotiated autonomy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Enslaved and formerly enslaved people created forms of resistance and negotiated autonomy.
questions.explanation: Maroons escaped plantations, used difficult terrain, and negotiated for land and freedom.

questions.prompt: Originalized essay by a Filipino nationalist reformer, c. 1890: “Spain praises liberty in its constitution, yet in the islands a student is watched, a newspaper is seized, and a native official is mocked for asking equal law. We seek representation, education, and dignity, not silence.” Which audience was the essay most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Nomadic rulers controlling Central Asian caravan routes
choices.B: Spanish liberals and educated Filipinos debating colonial reform
choices.C: Japanese military officials occupying Southeast Asia
choices.D: Factory owners defending laissez-faire labor contracts

questions.correctAnswerText: Spanish liberals and educated Filipinos debating colonial reform
questions.explanation: The essay appeals to liberal constitutional ideals and colonial reform debates involving Spain and educated Filipinos.

questions.prompt: Originalized essay by a Filipino nationalist reformer, c. 1890: “Spain praises liberty in its constitution, yet in the islands a student is watched, a newspaper is seized, and a native official is mocked for asking equal law. We seek representation, education, and dignity, not silence.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the author criticized unequal colonial citizenship?

choices.A: “A native official is mocked for asking equal law”
choices.B: “Spain praises liberty”
choices.C: “A newspaper is seized”
choices.D: “We seek education”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A native official is mocked for asking equal law”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly criticizes unequal colonial citizenship and legal treatment.

questions.prompt: Originalized essay by a Filipino nationalist reformer, c. 1890: “Spain praises liberty in its constitution, yet in the islands a student is watched, a newspaper is seized, and a native official is mocked for asking equal law. We seek representation, education, and dignity, not silence.” Which broader context best explains the essay?

choices.A: The disappearance of colonial rule in Southeast Asia before 1800
choices.B: The decline of print culture in nationalist movements
choices.C: The spread of plantation slavery to the Philippines
choices.D: The growth of educated colonial elites who used liberal ideas to challenge imperial inequality

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of educated colonial elites who used liberal ideas to challenge imperial inequality
questions.explanation: The essay uses education, representation, rights, and print criticism to challenge colonial inequality.

questions.prompt: Originalized essay by a Filipino nationalist reformer, c. 1890: “Spain praises liberty in its constitution, yet in the islands a student is watched, a newspaper is seized, and a native official is mocked for asking equal law. We seek representation, education, and dignity, not silence.” The reference to a seized newspaper most directly connects the essay to which development?

choices.A: The replacement of print culture by radio broadcasting before 1800
choices.B: The use of newspapers and print networks to circulate nationalist criticism
choices.C: The decline of literacy among colonial elites
choices.D: The creation of online social media campaigns

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of newspapers and print networks to circulate nationalist criticism
questions.explanation: Seized newspapers show the importance of print culture to nationalist reform movements.

questions.prompt:
Table: Industrial output index, 1870 = 100
Region | 1870 | 1910
Britain | 100 | 190
Germany | 100 | 420
Japan | 100 | 310
Russia | 100 | 360
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Industrial growth spread beyond Britain and accelerated in several late-industrializing states.
choices.B: Britain was the only country to industrialize before 1910.
choices.C: Japan and Russia experienced no industrial growth.
choices.D: Industrialization ended in Europe after 1870.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial growth spread beyond Britain and accelerated in several late-industrializing states.
questions.explanation: Germany, Japan, and Russia show large increases in industrial output by 1910.

questions.prompt:
Table: Industrial output index, 1870 = 100
Region | 1870 | 1910
Britain | 100 | 190
Germany | 100 | 420
Japan | 100 | 310
Russia | 100 | 360
The pattern shown in the table most directly contributed to which development?

choices.A: The end of military competition among industrial states
choices.B: The disappearance of urban labor forces
choices.C: The decline of state involvement in industrialization everywhere
choices.D: Intensified economic and military competition among industrial and industrializing powers

questions.correctAnswerText: Intensified economic and military competition among industrial and industrializing powers
questions.explanation: Industrial growth increased competition for markets, resources, military strength, and global influence.

questions.prompt:
Table: Industrial output index, 1870 = 100
Region | 1870 | 1910
Britain | 100 | 190
Germany | 100 | 420
Japan | 100 | 310
Russia | 100 | 360
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Industrialization was a fixed British monopoly throughout the nineteenth century.
choices.B: State and economic modernization in several regions narrowed Britain’s early industrial lead.
choices.C: Industrial output declined in every listed region.
choices.D: Industrialization was unrelated to global power shifts.

questions.correctAnswerText: State and economic modernization in several regions narrowed Britain’s early industrial lead.
questions.explanation: The stronger index growth in Germany, Japan, and Russia suggests Britain’s relative lead narrowed.

questions.prompt:
Table: Industrial output index, 1870 = 100
Region | 1870 | 1910
Britain | 100 | 190
Germany | 100 | 420
Japan | 100 | 310
Russia | 100 | 360
Which social change was commonly associated with the industrial growth shown?

choices.A: The disappearance of urban populations
choices.B: The universal end of class conflict
choices.C: The growth of urban working classes and new labor movements
choices.D: The replacement of wage labor by serfdom

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of urban working classes and new labor movements
questions.explanation: Industrial expansion commonly produced larger urban working classes and labor activism.

questions.prompt:
Table: Malaya tin economy, simplified colonial pattern
Period | Tin exports | Labor pattern | Infrastructure
1870s | Rising | Local and Chinese migrant labor | River transport
1900s | High | Large Chinese migrant workforce | Rail links to ports
1920s | Very high | Wage labor under colonial regulation | Dredges and export docks
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Industrial demand helped expand mining, migrant labor, and export infrastructure in Malaya.
choices.B: Malaya avoided integration into global industrial markets.
choices.C: Tin exports declined as colonial infrastructure expanded.
choices.D: Chinese migrant labor disappeared before mining intensified.

questions.correctAnswerText: Industrial demand helped expand mining, migrant labor, and export infrastructure in Malaya.
questions.explanation: The table links rising tin exports, migrant labor, rail links, dredges, and export docks.

questions.prompt:
Table: Malaya tin economy, simplified colonial pattern
Period | Tin exports | Labor pattern | Infrastructure
1870s | Rising | Local and Chinese migrant labor | River transport
1900s | High | Large Chinese migrant workforce | Rail links to ports
1920s | Very high | Wage labor under colonial regulation | Dredges and export docks
Which evidence best supports the claim that mining became more technologically intensive?

choices.A: “River transport”
choices.B: “Local and Chinese migrant labor”
choices.C: “Dredges and export docks”
choices.D: “Rising”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Dredges and export docks”
questions.explanation: Dredges and export docks show more technologically intensive mining and export infrastructure.

questions.prompt:
Table: Malaya tin economy, simplified colonial pattern
Period | Tin exports | Labor pattern | Infrastructure
1870s | Rising | Local and Chinese migrant labor | River transport
1900s | High | Large Chinese migrant workforce | Rail links to ports
1920s | Very high | Wage labor under colonial regulation | Dredges and export docks
Which broader context best explains the labor pattern shown?

choices.A: The end of migration within Asia after 1750
choices.B: The replacement of mining by subsistence farming
choices.C: The refusal of colonial economies to use wage labor
choices.D: The movement of workers across Asia in response to expanding colonial labor markets

questions.correctAnswerText: The movement of workers across Asia in response to expanding colonial labor markets
questions.explanation: Chinese migrant labor in Malaya reflects Asian labor migration driven by colonial economic expansion.

questions.prompt:
Table: Malaya tin economy, simplified colonial pattern
Period | Tin exports | Labor pattern | Infrastructure
1870s | Rising | Local and Chinese migrant labor | River transport
1900s | High | Large Chinese migrant workforce | Rail links to ports
1920s | Very high | Wage labor under colonial regulation | Dredges and export docks
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Colonial economies avoided regulating migrant labor.
choices.B: Colonial export economies could combine resource extraction, migrant labor, and transportation infrastructure.
choices.C: Industrialization reduced demand for Southeast Asian raw materials.
choices.D: Mining economies were independent of global markets.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial export economies could combine resource extraction, migrant labor, and transportation infrastructure.
questions.explanation: Tin mining relied on migrant workers, rail links, dredges, and export docks.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by Queen Liliʻuokalani of Hawaiʻi, 1890s: “Our land is not a plantation ledger to be balanced by foreign planters. The constitution forced upon us silences native voices while sugar men speak of order and prosperity. I protest in the name of our people and our sovereignty.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To challenge foreign planter influence and defend Hawaiian sovereignty
choices.B: To invite overseas planters to abolish the monarchy peacefully
choices.C: To support the annexation of Hawaiʻi by foreign sugar companies
choices.D: To reject all Indigenous political claims

questions.correctAnswerText: To challenge foreign planter influence and defend Hawaiian sovereignty
questions.explanation: The statement protests foreign planter power, imposed constitutional restrictions, and threats to sovereignty.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by Queen Liliʻuokalani of Hawaiʻi, 1890s: “Our land is not a plantation ledger to be balanced by foreign planters. The constitution forced upon us silences native voices while sugar men speak of order and prosperity. I protest in the name of our people and our sovereignty.” The statement most directly connects imperial expansion to which economic interest?

choices.A: Fur tribute across Siberia
choices.B: Tin mining in Southeast Asia
choices.C: Textile production in Manchester
choices.D: Sugar plantation profits and commercial influence

questions.correctAnswerText: Sugar plantation profits and commercial influence
questions.explanation: The reference to planters, sugar men, and plantation ledgers connects imperial pressure to sugar interests.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by Queen Liliʻuokalani of Hawaiʻi, 1890s: “Our land is not a plantation ledger to be balanced by foreign planters. The constitution forced upon us silences native voices while sugar men speak of order and prosperity. I protest in the name of our people and our sovereignty.” Which phrase best supports the claim that political rights were restricted?

choices.A: “Sugar men”
choices.B: “Silences native voices”
choices.C: “Order and prosperity”
choices.D: “Plantation ledger”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Silences native voices”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly points to restricted political participation by Native Hawaiians.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by Queen Liliʻuokalani of Hawaiʻi, 1890s: “Our land is not a plantation ledger to be balanced by foreign planters. The constitution forced upon us silences native voices while sugar men speak of order and prosperity. I protest in the name of our people and our sovereignty.” Which statement best evaluates the argument?

choices.A: The author claims that plantation profits should determine sovereignty.
choices.B: The author argues that Indigenous political authority should be replaced by foreign constitutions.
choices.C: The author frames foreign economic influence as a threat to Indigenous sovereignty and political voice.
choices.D: The author denies that land had any political meaning.

questions.correctAnswerText: The author frames foreign economic influence as a threat to Indigenous sovereignty and political voice.
questions.explanation: The statement links plantation interests, a forced constitution, silenced Native voices, and sovereignty.

questions.prompt: Originalized survivor testimony, Ottoman Anatolia, 1915: “The gendarmes read an order that our village must leave within two days. They said it was for security, but the old men were separated, the church was locked, and families were driven toward the desert with no food.” What was the most likely purpose of preserving such testimony?

choices.A: To document the experiences of victims of deportation and mass violence
choices.B: To praise Ottoman wartime security policy
choices.C: To show that civilians were unaffected by World War I
choices.D: To prove that forced migration ended before the twentieth century

questions.correctAnswerText: To document the experiences of victims of deportation and mass violence
questions.explanation: The testimony records forced removal, separation, religious targeting, hunger, and deadly deportation.

questions.prompt: Originalized survivor testimony, Ottoman Anatolia, 1915: “The gendarmes read an order that our village must leave within two days. They said it was for security, but the old men were separated, the church was locked, and families were driven toward the desert with no food.” Which phrase most directly supports the claim that the policy targeted a civilian community?

choices.A: “They said it was for security”
choices.B: “Within two days”
choices.C: “The gendarmes read an order”
choices.D: “Our village must leave”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Our village must leave”
questions.explanation: The order applied to an entire village, showing targeting of a civilian community.

questions.prompt: Originalized survivor testimony, Ottoman Anatolia, 1915: “The gendarmes read an order that our village must leave within two days. They said it was for security, but the old men were separated, the church was locked, and families were driven toward the desert with no food.” Which statement best evaluates the official explanation described in the testimony?

choices.A: The testimony confirms that deportation was a voluntary resettlement program.
choices.B: The testimony challenges the security justification by describing separation, religious targeting, and deadly forced movement.
choices.C: The testimony shows that wartime violence was limited to soldiers.
choices.D: The testimony proves that all religious communities were treated identically.

questions.correctAnswerText: The testimony challenges the security justification by describing separation, religious targeting, and deadly forced movement.
questions.explanation: The testimony contrasts official “security” claims with violence against civilians and religious institutions.

questions.prompt:
Table: Soviet wartime relocation, simplified figures
Category | Approximate pattern, 1941–1942
Factories moved east | More than 1,000
Workers and technicians moved | Millions
Key destinations | Urals, Siberia, Central Asia
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: World War II ended industrial production in the Soviet Union.
choices.B: Soviet wartime industry remained entirely in western borderlands.
choices.C: Total war required large-scale state coordination of industry and labor.
choices.D: Industrial relocation was unrelated to military survival.

questions.correctAnswerText: Total war required large-scale state coordination of industry and labor.
questions.explanation: Moving factories and millions of workers required centralized wartime planning.

questions.prompt:
Table: Soviet wartime relocation, simplified figures
Category | Approximate pattern, 1941–1942
Factories moved east | More than 1,000
Workers and technicians moved | Millions
Key destinations | Urals, Siberia, Central Asia
The pattern in the table most directly reflects which broader feature of World War II?

choices.A: Governments mobilized economies, populations, and technology for military survival.
choices.B: States reduced economic planning during wartime.
choices.C: Civilian labor became irrelevant to military production.
choices.D: The war eliminated the importance of industrial capacity.

questions.correctAnswerText: Governments mobilized economies, populations, and technology for military survival.
questions.explanation: Soviet factory relocation shows total war’s demands on industry, labor, and state organization.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Gamal Abdel Nasser, Egypt, 1956: “The canal was dug by Egyptian labor and watered by Egyptian blood. Its revenue shall build our dam and our schools. We do not reject the world, but we reject a company that rules our river road from abroad.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To invite Britain and France to restore direct colonial control
choices.B: To justify nationalizing the Suez Canal as an act of sovereignty and development
choices.C: To reject all state involvement in economic planning
choices.D: To oppose Egyptian control over infrastructure

questions.correctAnswerText: To justify nationalizing the Suez Canal as an act of sovereignty and development
questions.explanation: Nasser links the canal to Egyptian labor, revenue, development, and rejection of foreign company control.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Gamal Abdel Nasser, Egypt, 1956: “The canal was dug by Egyptian labor and watered by Egyptian blood. Its revenue shall build our dam and our schools. We do not reject the world, but we reject a company that rules our river road from abroad.” Which phrase best supports the claim that economic development motivated the policy?

choices.A: “Egyptian blood”
choices.B: “We do not reject the world”
choices.C: “Rules our river road”
choices.D: “Its revenue shall build our dam and our schools”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Its revenue shall build our dam and our schools”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects canal revenue to development projects.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Gamal Abdel Nasser, Egypt, 1956: “The canal was dug by Egyptian labor and watered by Egyptian blood. Its revenue shall build our dam and our schools. We do not reject the world, but we reject a company that rules our river road from abroad.” Which broader context best explains the speech?

choices.A: The decline of Arab nationalism after World War I
choices.B: The return of European colonial rule without resistance after 1945
choices.C: Postcolonial leaders’ efforts to control strategic resources and assert national dignity
choices.D: The disappearance of Cold War competition in the Middle East

questions.correctAnswerText: Postcolonial leaders’ efforts to control strategic resources and assert national dignity
questions.explanation: The speech reflects postcolonial nationalism, control of infrastructure, and economic development.

questions.prompt: Described Chinese propaganda poster, early 1950s: “Peasants stand in front of a landlord’s former estate holding title papers and farm tools. A red banner reads, ‘Land to those who till it.’ A cadre points toward a village meeting table.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Communist governments used land redistribution to mobilize rural support and transform social hierarchy.
choices.B: Communist states rejected peasant mobilization.
choices.C: Land reform protected landlord privileges.
choices.D: The poster shows a policy unrelated to political ideology.

questions.correctAnswerText: Communist governments used land redistribution to mobilize rural support and transform social hierarchy.
questions.explanation: Peasants, title papers, a landlord estate, and the slogan show land redistribution as political mobilization.

questions.prompt: Described Chinese propaganda poster, early 1950s: “Peasants stand in front of a landlord’s former estate holding title papers and farm tools. A red banner reads, ‘Land to those who till it.’ A cadre points toward a village meeting table.” The poster reflects which broader post-1945 development?

choices.A: The global return of feudal landlordism without challenge
choices.B: Communist revolutionary states using social reform to consolidate power
choices.C: The end of rural politics in Asia
choices.D: The spread of liberal constitutional monarchy across China

questions.correctAnswerText: Communist revolutionary states using social reform to consolidate power
questions.explanation: Land reform was a major way communist states built rural support and transformed society.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global apparel production and waste, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1990 | c. 2020
Major export production | Concentrated in fewer regions | Spread across more low-cost manufacturing zones
Average collection size by brands | Smaller | Larger and faster-changing
Textile waste reported by cities | Lower | Higher
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Clothing production became less global after 1990.
choices.B: Textile waste disappeared as fashion markets expanded.
choices.C: Fast-changing global apparel supply chains increased production and waste pressures.
choices.D: Apparel production remained limited to one industrial country.

questions.correctAnswerText: Fast-changing global apparel supply chains increased production and waste pressures.
questions.explanation: More manufacturing zones, faster collections, and higher textile waste point to fast-fashion globalization.

questions.prompt: Originalized NGO report, c. 2018: “A shirt designed in Europe may be sewn in Bangladesh from fabric shipped through East Asia, sold online in North America, and discarded months later in a landfill or secondhand market. Workers ask who benefits when speed is rewarded and wages are squeezed.” What was the most likely purpose of the report?

choices.A: To criticize labor and environmental problems in global fast-fashion supply chains
choices.B: To argue that global trade had ended before 2000
choices.C: To promote the return of mercantilist chartered companies
choices.D: To deny that consumers are connected to overseas production

questions.correctAnswerText: To criticize labor and environmental problems in global fast-fashion supply chains
questions.explanation: The report traces a global supply chain and criticizes speed, low wages, and disposal.

questions.prompt: Originalized NGO report, c. 2018: “A shirt designed in Europe may be sewn in Bangladesh from fabric shipped through East Asia, sold online in North America, and discarded months later in a landfill or secondhand market. Workers ask who benefits when speed is rewarded and wages are squeezed.” Which phrase best supports the claim that digital commerce shaped the supply chain?

choices.A: “Fabric shipped through East Asia”
choices.B: “Wages are squeezed”
choices.C: “Discarded months later”
choices.D: “Sold online in North America”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Sold online in North America”
questions.explanation: Online sales directly show digital commerce shaping the supply chain.

questions.prompt: Originalized NGO report, c. 2018: “A shirt designed in Europe may be sewn in Bangladesh from fabric shipped through East Asia, sold online in North America, and discarded months later in a landfill or secondhand market. Workers ask who benefits when speed is rewarded and wages are squeezed.” Which environmental concern is most directly connected to the report?

choices.A: The end of landfill use in consumer societies
choices.B: Waste and pollution created by rapid production and disposal of consumer goods
choices.C: The disappearance of synthetic fibers after 1990
choices.D: The decline of global transportation emissions

questions.correctAnswerText: Waste and pollution created by rapid production and disposal of consumer goods
questions.explanation: The report mentions fast production and disposal in landfills or secondhand markets.

questions.prompt: Originalized NGO report, c. 2018: “A shirt designed in Europe may be sewn in Bangladesh from fabric shipped through East Asia, sold online in North America, and discarded months later in a landfill or secondhand market. Workers ask who benefits when speed is rewarded and wages are squeezed.” Which statement best evaluates the report’s argument?

choices.A: It argues that globalization eliminated unequal labor conditions.
choices.B: It claims that consumers are isolated from production networks.
choices.C: It links global consumer culture to labor exploitation, rapid production, and environmental waste.
choices.D: It rejects the idea that supply chains cross national borders.

questions.correctAnswerText: It links global consumer culture to labor exploitation, rapid production, and environmental waste.
questions.explanation: The report connects design, production, online sales, squeezed wages, and disposal across regions.
